caveats: a: Technology adoption is measured by acreage or farms, not share of labor time.
a: Rice systems differ materially between the Mid-South and California.
rho: No representative autonomous-task completion dataset was located.
rho: Prototype irrigation systems still report installation, maintenance, and reliability challenges.
e: Repeat sales to a mill or cooperative are not, by themselves, outsourced services.
e: Custom-work revenue may be reported outside the core establishment classification.
s5: The source measures farmland acres rather than rice-farm firms.
s5: The transfer evidence is older and national across all agricultural land.
q: Water access and land scarcity differ sharply by region.
q: Government programs can alter downside protection without creating firm-specific advantage.
d5: The latest outlook is a one-year forecast rather than a five-year projection.
d5: Sales-volume exposure to exports increases sensitivity to trade and foreign crop conditions.
o: Custom operators can perform field tasks, but the accountable farming enterprise remains necessary.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is predominantly seasonal sales of an agricultural commodity or seed to mills, merchandisers, cooperatives, and other buyers; contracted farming or management revenue may occur but is not the typical model.
lens.excluded_activities: Wild-rice farming, mixed grain or oilseed farms not classified to rice, rice milling and distribution, hobby or subsistence operations, passive farmland ownership, and captive production without meaningful third-party revenue.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market owner-operated, tenant-operated, and sharecropped farms primarily growing rice other than wild rice or producing rice seed, with recurring third-party crop sales and any genuinely contracted cultivation or water-management services identified at the firm level.

a.bridge: USDA documents automated guidance on well over half of U.S. rice acreage and lower adoption of yield maps, soil maps, and variable-rate technologies, while rice-specific research demonstrates remote sensing and automated irrigation monitoring. I translate this demonstrated digital surface into addressable labor in crop planning, irrigation decisions, scouting interpretation, input prescription, recordkeeping, marketing, and administration; the proxy likely overstates substitution where connectivity, field variability, and hands-on intervention remain binding.
caveats: Technology adoption is measured by acreage or farms, not share of labor time.
Rice systems differ materially between the Mid-South and California.
a.change_evidence: A rice-specific task-hour study by farm size, irrigation system, and technology stack would materially change a.
a.rationale: Information-intensive planning and monitoring are meaningfully addressable, but land preparation, planting, irrigation repair, chemical and nutrient application, harvesting, equipment service, and weather response remain physical and site-dependent.

d5.bridge: USDA reports that global markets account for 40–45% of annual sales volume of U.S.-produced rice and its July 2026 outlook projects a 39-year-low 2026/27 crop from reduced area alongside continued domestic consumption and exports. I translate this combination of enduring food demand, export exposure, and volatile domestic acreage into a flat central five-year real demand multiplier with a wide range; the current supply contraction could make the base too conservative if acreage normalizes or too optimistic if U.S. export competitiveness erodes.
caveats: The latest outlook is a one-year forecast rather than a five-year projection.
Sales-volume exposure to exports increases sensitivity to trade and foreign crop conditions.
d5.change_evidence: USDA long-term projections decomposed into U.S. rice volume, real farm price, domestic use, exports, and regional acreage would materially change d5.
d5.rationale: Rice is a staple with durable underlying use, but U.S. growers face acreage swings, drought, water limits, global competition, trade conditions, and commodity-price cycles.

caveats: Repeat sales to a mill or cooperative are not, by themselves, outsourced services.
Custom-work revenue may be reported outside the core establishment classification.
e.change_evidence: Farm-level revenue splits between crop sales, seed sales, custom farming, and ongoing management contracts would materially change e.
e.rationale: Census defines the industry as growing rice or producing rice seed, so the economic output is overwhelmingly a product rather than a recurring outsourced service. A small tail of contract cultivation or farm-management arrangements may qualify.

l.detail: SUSB scope excludes this activity, no IPS labor-compensation/output series at any level, and no other receipts source downloaded; labor_share left null (recorded gap).
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $299,458 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 11); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Custom operators can perform field tasks, but the accountable farming enterprise remains necessary.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of scaled, fully managed autonomous rice-production platforms that remove nearly all operating responsibility from the owner would materially change o.
o.rationale: Even with autonomous guidance and irrigation decision support, a farm operator must control land access, water, equipment, biological production risk, compliance, financing, crop insurance, and physical execution through the season.

caveats: Water access and land scarcity differ sharply by region.
Government programs can alter downside protection without creating firm-specific advantage.
q.change_evidence: Farm-level yield and cost persistence, water-right security, acreage concentration, buyer concentration, and margin dispersion across cycles would materially change q.
q.rationale: Suitable leveled land, reliable water, local infrastructure, scale, agronomic know-how, and buyer relationships create barriers, but rice is a traded commodity exposed to benchmark pricing, policy, weather, input costs, and domestic and international competition.

caveats: No representative autonomous-task completion dataset was located.
Prototype irrigation systems still report installation, maintenance, and reliability challenges.
rho.change_evidence: Observed rice-farm deployment results reporting autonomous completion, exception, override, and error rates by workflow would materially change rho.
rho.rationale: Within addressable work, routine monitoring, guidance, documentation, forecasting, and decision support can be highly automated, while agronomic exceptions, water-control failures, buyer negotiations, risk decisions, and safety-critical equipment actions retain human review.

s5.bridge: USDA's farmland-transfer study estimated that only 2.3% of farmland would be sold to nonrelatives over five years, while other land could transfer through relatives, trusts, wills, or gifts. I use the thin open-market land supply as a lower anchor and expand the range because an operating farm can transact through leases, equipment, entities, or family succession without an arm's-length land sale; the acreage-based proxy may understate firm availability but may overstate it where land changes hands without a saleable operating platform.
caveats: The source measures farmland acres rather than rice-farm firms.
The transfer evidence is older and national across all agricultural land.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year rice-farm control transactions, including entity sales, long-term leases, equipment transfers, and family successions, divided by the eligible LMM operator population would materially change s5.
s5.rationale: Farmland is illiquid and family transfer is common, limiting conventional control acquisitions even though operator aging and lease structures can create entry points.

principal_driver: Established rice operations combine scarce suitable land and water with a proven precision-agriculture base that can support incremental AI productivity.
principal_weakness: Rice farming sells a volatile physical commodity rather than a recurring outsourced service, leaving very little lens-eligible revenue.
